CURRENT AFFAIRS.

The A.O.H. have acted wisely in abandoning its usual
parades and demonstrations on the Feast of the As-
sumption, August 15th. The secretary, Mr. Nugent,
says in the official letter announcing the decision of the
National Board of the A.O.H.:— It has now become
notorious that the Unionist wirepullers in Ulster are
endeavouring to create riot and disorder, these being Hz'e
only arguments they have against Home Rule. It is
therefore our duty to deny them any faciliiies.” This
is a courageous decision, for it always requires
courage to refrain, owing to higher considera-
tions, from doing what you wusually do and

-what you are legally entitled to do. Nothing would
please the '‘Irish Times ' better than that some
A.O.H. parades should be the occasion for Orange at-
tacks, for if an A.O.H. procession were attacked it
would probably have to defend itself, and then the lying
cry would go up that the A.O.H. had attacked unoffend-
ing Unionists, The Unionists are in a low-down dirty
game; and the common-sense and self-restraint dis-
played in. the decision of the No:tlonnl Board of the
.0.H. is most creditable ard satisfactory. -

Recently a public corporation in Canada sent us an
advertisement order which we could not see our way
to accept owing to our attitude towards the emigration
evil. We replied stating so and we must say the com-
pany took the matter very well and sent us the follow-
ing rather flattering letter:—'* Editor, LEADER. ‘ Dare
to do right.” Sir,—Two months ago we sent you copy
for a quarter of a page advertisement, and subsequently
sent an additional quarter page copy, or half page in
all, which we intended to run continuously. The matter
consisted in part of offering farms in Canada, all in
working order, to Irishmen on easy terms; and our
plan was to colonize out here, and then let the men re
turn after making money enough to start business in
Ireland Hence the advertisement could be looked
upon as inducing the Irishmen to leave home. On the
8th of June you returned us a P.O. order, and in thank-
ing us for patronage declined the advertisement be-
cause of the fact that it would foster emigration from
Ireland. This letter is to inform you that while we
regret your decision, we most highly appreciate your
honesty of purpose. There is nothing like ‘ acting the
man,’ and that the LeaDER has done <o in this instance
is only one more proof that it lives up to its motto, viz.,
‘ Ireland for the Irish.” Your patriotism is worthy of
emulation. Time prevents us saying more at present,
so we conclude with kindest regards, hats in hand.—
Yours faithfully, St. C. B. and I. Co., Limited, Sarnia,
Canada, 22nd July, 1012.”

‘“ Address and testimonial of the people of Toura-
neena and Ballinamult to Sergeant O’Flaherty on the
occasion of his departure from Ballinamult.”” Such is
the heading to what appeats to be an advertisement in
a local paper. Where is Ballinamult ? In the days of
our grandchildren no doubt that.question can be an-
swered by any school child in Ireland—Ballinamult, of
course, is famous as the place where policeman O’Fla-
herty got a testimonial. These addresses are invari-
ably amusing, notwithstanding their frequency. Here
is the second paragraph :—

Your anxiety for the peace of the district, your
‘untiring zeal and energy, your promptness in ur-
gency, your common sense and tact in dealing with
crowds, rendered you a most efficient, in fact, an
ideal peace officer, and earned for you the respect
and regard of all law-abiding inhabitants.

The address says nothing about O’Flaherty in his
capacity as a Bung catcher. We are left in doubt as
to his standing in that connection ; but as we understand
4two of the persons who signed the address are Bungs,
it is probable that O’Flaherty never went up a tree,
went down among the rushes, nor used field glasses in
his endeavour to *‘ spot ” a Bung amidst the breaking
of the law.

Peeler O’Flaherty must have felt a load on him until
he had composed his reply to the dutiful address of his
subjects in Ballinamult. ~Polishing boots and creasing
one’s hair are routine works, and even playing a con-
certina or fishing for trout are rather commonplace acti-
vities in the life of a country bobby, but it was now up
to Sergeant O'Flaherty to compose a grand composi-

tion. We give the first paragraph :— T8

My Dear Friends:—I don’t think I have ever
felt at such a disadvantage in my life. Your too
kind and flattering address and your generous testi-
monial are, I feel, entirely undeserved by me.
That such marks of appreciation {o those in my
office are not usual, gives me a pardonable pride

T - e

606 THE LEADER.

and these acts can only be the outcome of a kindly
disposed andi(if you allow me to use the words of
your address) a law-abiding people.
Surely even a'Privy Councillor could not have done 16
better! But where is Ballinamult ?

BUNG AND MODERN TACTICS.

A peeler hiding up a tree,
Or in the rushes oh,
Was surely bad enough for m2
And thirsty friends also;
But when a bobby with a glass
Can watch a drinking shop
A mile away or more, alas,
It is a bitter drop. *

A plague on glasses for the eye,
That distance makes so clear,
And.helps a-peeler to descry
Bung’s trade in Sunday beer.
Such things are useful to enhance
The view of race or star,
But ne’er should aid a bobby’s glance
In watching drinking bar.

Oh science, science, I do fear
When peelers thus you aid,

You sound the doom of Sunday beer
And. Bung's illicit trade.

What with X-rays and air machines,
That fly the clouds among,

You may give bobbies dreadful means
To persecute poor Bung.

A. M. W

The following letter from Johannesburg will interest
our readers, more particularly those of them who are
hurlers :—*‘ Johannesburg, July 8th, 1912. The Editor
of the LEaDER. Dear Sir,—Judging by some correspon-
dence’ which. has appeared in' your valuable paper re-
cently, it would appear that some people in Ireland are
ashamed of the national game of hurling In order {o
show that this feeling is not shared by the Irish in this
country, I am:sending.to you (under separafe cover) a
copy of the ¢ Transvaal Leader,”” which contains a ve-
port: of our hurling. meetings, not that this-is anything
unusual, as-the papers here frequently contain refer-
ence to the game, as well as'photos:of the teams; snap-

- shots of the game in progress, etc. Hurling is played
here every Sunday afternoon on the most fashionable
sports ground.in the city, and attracts large crowds of
spectators of all nationalities: The ground on which
we" play was formerly owned. by the late President
Kruger and was presented by him to the municipality
of Johannesburg, and is now leased as a sports ground.
The majority of our players are of course Irishmen, but
we have also some South Africans, Americans and Aus-

- tralians. Practically our only difficulty is with hurleys.
“We-cannot_procure any in this country, and the firm

in Cork from which we have been getting them usually
take four or five months after having received. our order
before sending out the goods. ~We send our orders
througli the London agents of Messrs. John Orr and
Co., a prominent Irish firm here, and are satisfied to
pay the top price for the right article. If there is any
Trish firm which would like to have our orders for hur-
leys and balls and who would be prepared to give
prompt attention to same, I should be pleased to hear
from them. Hurling has also been played in Kimber-
ley, Cape Colony, for many years. Last year a team
from there travelled to Johannesburg, a journey of

Avcust 10, 1912.

about fourteen hours by rail. Lhis journey was made

at considerable inconvenience and expense, and without -

any remuneration, as the laws of this country forbids
gate money to be taken on Sundays. A return mateh.
will be played at Kimberley this year. Hurling has
lately been introduced into Natal by a body of Irlﬁh-
men who came out from the Arklow branch of Kynoch’s,
Ltd., to that firm’s branch near Durban, Nat{\.l. \Ve.
expect to send a team from here to play against one of
their teams in the near future.—-L remain, sir, your
obedient servant, Nicholas Joyce, Hon Sec., Transvaal
Huling Association.”

The Orange Falstaffs who were full of wind and windy
thunder a while ago are now skeddadling in fear. The
Grand Master of the Belfast Orangemen, one Colonel
Wallace, even attempts-in:a lefter or manifesto to place
the discredit of the Belfast atrocities on ‘ irresponsible
lads.”” The Belfast atrocities have keen so atrocious
that they have gained the ear of Eingland, and the whole
gang who thought to profit by them, from Carson down
to the ‘“ Irish Times,’” are in a desperate funk.

The licking of the ‘‘ Trish Times.’’ to heel: goes on.
On Thursday it said :—* We think that they (the Bel-
fast atrocities) are a disgrace to Belfast, that they can
be excused by no fervour of political fecling, and that
they ought to be promptly and firmly suppressed.’”” The
““ Trish Times "’ has had a rather sudden stop put to its
gallop. Later on it refers to “ the brutal attacks on
Roman Catholic workmen,’’ and it describes the Bel-
fast atrocities as ‘* deplorable and inexcusable.” The
““Trvish Times "’ is like a rat in a trap. tlere is its*
apology for the murderous Belfast blackguards. The
apology takes the form of a polite negative. Tt is
‘* The uneducated and: desperately earnest shipworke:
of Belfast are not carved in alabaster.”” We agree with
‘“Al£”" inrthat : it seems to us that they are carved ouf
of Belfast refuse and filth. But all the while that
““ Alf ”’ is throwing over the Belfast hlackguards, it 1s
edging to make political bluster out of them :—‘ Ulster
still remains—a. grim note of interrogation. ~ What is
Mr. Asquith going to do about Ulster?” As our
readers know, and as even ‘“Alf "’ knows, Ulster is
about half Nationalist, so that the using of the term
Ulster in this' connection is a deliberate fraud
on the part of Amott’s ‘ Times.”” The small
rowdy band of protesters against Home Rule,
including the braves of . the Belfast atrocities—
the vast majority of present day Unionists will
no doubt acquiesce quietly to a reform passed by
the Crown and Imperial Parliament—will have to do
when Home Rule is law what they have to do now
with regard to, say, taxes, they will have to grin and
bear it. If the rowdy element comes out against Home
Rule as the Belfast blackguards came out against un-
fortunate Catholic workmen and Protestant Home Rule
workmen, three battalions, and if necessary thir6y bat-
talions, of his- Most Graciqus Majesty’s troops must be
sent to keep them in order. There is nothing like a
Tommy with a loaded rifle for keeping certain criminals
and Orange blackguards cool.

Evidently some Unionists think that the question,
*“ What will Asquith do with ‘ Ulster’ ’? is a poser.
Of course Asquith will do what the Government has at
length:done to cope with the preliminary ‘Civil War, *
he will'send His Majesty’s soldiers to help the police to
put down blackguardism, riot and disorder. What else
could:he do. A more pertinent question for the  Trish
Times” and others to be asking themselves is, ““ What
will Ireland do to them if Treland docs not get Home

Rule *’? A simple person might be led to believe that
the race that went through the Penal Laws and through
landlordism can do nothing at all if the cup of Self-
l(z‘rovemm_ent is dashed from their lips! Perhaps the
Irish Times * had better consider that phase of the
future possibilities.

The Belfast blackguards are the Unionists’ trump
cards, and they throw them away reluctantly and, we
have no doubt, will pick them up off the fioor to be
played again at the first convenient opportunity. Even
whilst the *“ Irish Times * has to denounce them it uses
them. For instance, in its article of August 1st, in
which it was forced to say that the atrocities were a
disgrace to Belfast, it yet drags the blackguards, the

irresponsible lads,”” as Colonel Wallace pretends to
regard them, to its aid as a political force. It says:—

The Belfast riots, with their cruelty and injus-
tice, are the first results of Mr. Asquith’s attempt
to crush a strong-necked people. During the next
two years political feeling will run terribly high in
Ulster. We must hope, but we dare mot believe,
that further violent outbreaks may be averted.

So the vile blackguardism, and the prospect of further
blackguardism during the next two years, arve held out
as a reason why the Imperial Government should be
intimidated from doing what it has a constitutional
right to do: at the whiz of the Orange rivet and at the
slash of the Orange knife the ‘‘ Trish Times *’ suggests
that the Tmperial Government should blanch and stand
and deliver! To such a position is the *‘ Irish Times’”
driven. A few columns removed from this article in
the ‘‘ Irish Times’ we see an advertisement of St.
Vincent's College, Castleknock; but Ireland may mot
always be so tame as to lick the hand that tries to smite
it and libel it.

There was a’ Unionist meeting in Kells, and one of
the ranters was a ‘“ playboy "’ from the Abbey Theatre
coterie, an opal hush dramatist, Lord Dunsany. In
his speech he said he was not a politician! We know
that type. You will find plenty of them backing up
the Hairy Fairies of the I.A.0.S. and of Plunkett House
and standing up in the cause of “hberty’” for the
Pegeen Mike campaign. We never sat out one of Lord
Dunsany’s alleged plays, but we understand on hearsay
that they are rather intangible dramas. In his capa-
city as a Unionist stump orator we do not think much
of him.

An anti-Home Rule meeting, organised by the Fer-
managh branch of the Ulster Women's Unionist Asso-
ciation, was held in Enniskillen. According to one of
the speakers the Ulster Unionist Women'’s Association
now numbered 100,000 members. We think we have
heard that number before. Is it mot also the number-of
farmers organised by the Hairy Fairies ?

Bonar Law, the leader of the Unionist Party, s(.t\l.\ds
avowedly for treason and rebellion.- Tt is & very serious
state of-affairs.  Mr. Asquith, who measures .11is words,
said with reference to Mr. Bonar Law's position :——

The right hon. gentleman tells us that if this
Parliament should see fit in the exercise of what
he admits to be well within its legal competence,
to pass a Home Rule Bill into law in the course of

THE LEADER.- 607

the next two years, in his opinion, his deliberate
opinion, it would be the: right of the minority of
the people in Ireland to resist the application of
that measure by force.

The Prime Minister put the position of the ““ loyal "
and Imperial Mr. Law very plainly when he said :—

The moment you lay down, as the leaders of the
Constitutional party lay down, the doctrine that a
minority, I do not care what minority—if you like,
a majority—are entitled, because a particular Act
of legislation is distasteful to their views, and, as
they think, oppressive to their interests, to resist
it by force, there is an absolute end to Parliamen-
tary government.

It is a good thing for us that a third-rate politician
and a blunderer like Law is leader of the Unionists.
We hope they will stick to him. But we fear that an
L.M.G. movement cannot be long delayed.

In this year’s national competition of Schools of Arb
and Art Schools and Classes, the following awards have
been made in respect of works submitted by students
of schools working under the Department of Agricul-
ture and Technical Instruction for Ireland :—I gold
medal, 5 silver medals, 8 bronze medals, and 11 hook
prizes ; whilst 29 other works were commended by the
examiners. Mr. Clarke, for whose design for stained
glass the examiners award :a gold medal .and which they
characterise as a ** fine imaginative design,” is a stu-
dent of the Metropolitan School of Awt, Dublin. His
success is the more noteworthy as he was awarded a
gold medal for work in the same subject in last year’s
competition. The award of a gold medal indicates a
very high standard of excellence, gold ‘medals having
this year being given in respect of only 12 of the 11,247
works submitted for competition. In addition to the
gold medal, the students of the Metropolitan :School of
Arxt secured 4 silver medals, 5 bronze medals, 6 book
prizes and 14 commendations. We congratulate young
Mr. Clarke on his success and we may also take -occa-
sion to congratulate his father, whose stained glass and
church decoration work has made him so widely known
throughout Ireland. Our readers will remember that
Mr. Clarke was the contractor for the beautiful and
much-admired decorations of St. Andrew’s Church,
Westland Row. &

But now when his wails o’er Ulster’s dales

Oh Rudyard sing of Carson and Craig,

Mix them up in your muse’s cup,

Shove 'them out on your sing-song shout,

The LeapEr has a long reach and an effective one.
Very Rev. Canon Barrett, the President of St. Colman'’s,
encloses a lefter to us that came to him all the way
from the Philippine Islands. The letter covers a re-
mittance for a son of the writer’s for a year’s residence
and schooling in St. Colman’s College. ~ Our readers
will be interested in a few extracts from the letter:—
‘“ Reading that plucky little champion, the Dublin
LEADER, convinced me your College would be just the
place for my boy for four or five years (there are others
to follow, with God’s help, when the time comes). . . .""
Further on in his letter the father of the boy writes :—
* To an exile it surprises me how our people wobble on
yports. We that aim at nationhood, which I hope is
poming at last, should strive to make the little things
serve as foundation stones for big.  Hurling versus
cricket, Gaelic, pastimes versus British affectations.
The intelligent foreigner may say, the Irish are a
branch of the English people, I noticed they played
English games; or, the Irish are a distinct people from
the English, their games and pastimes are distinctly
Irish and National.  So kindly teach my youngster
neither an English game nor an English accent. o
The boy will be thirteen years of age on the 1st October,
1912. Send him back to me a good. Catholic
Rhristian, a sturdy Irishman, and a sound scholar and

ill be satisfied and ever thankful.”

Practical patronage of this kind from the ends of the
arth for an Irish College may convince some shallow
nd ignorantly sceptical ‘‘ bread and butter’’ people
that after all there is something tangible in being Irish.

Whilst & man who has seen the world sends his boy
from the far away Philippine Island to St. Colman's
College, Fermoy, many an ignorant man, and woman
also, in Ireland, whose ignorance is only slightly
veneered with such things as a moderate amount of
money can buy, thinks that nothing is good enough for
;ood-for-nothing * Chawley "’ but a non-Irish or an Eng-
ish-College.

Mr. W. J. Evans was appointed Chief Inspector for
Wales under the National Insurance Act. He has since
resigned. Protests were made by many Welsh societies,
aotably by the Cymmrodorion of Cardiff and Car-
marthen, against Mr. Evans’s appointment on.the score
of his haying no knowledge of the Welsh language.
And now he has resigned. But Wales is not Anglicised
The “ Western Mail,”’ of Cardiff, has a lead-
ing article on the matter in its issue of July 25th. Com-
menting on the resignation, it says:—

For some reasons the step he has taken is re-
grettable. The Commissioners had good reasons
for the selection of this gentleman, and no one

THE LEADER.

Avgusr 10, 1912..

denies that he has the talents which make a
capable officer.  His knowledge of public aﬁu.n_‘s
in Wales, in their later, humane, developments is
a qualification of no lititle importance. But there
was one drawbaclk to the appointment, and it was!
a drawback which gave offence to the nationalist
instinct which is so strong among the peo_ple of
Wales. Mr. W. J. Evans has not a colloquial ac-
quaintance with the Welsh language, and it has
been a subject of complaint that he would have
been debarred thereby from giving direct and per-
sonal attention to some of the duties of his im-
portant position. . . . The Commissioners an
have to make another appointment, and they will
appoint o Welsh-speaking Welshman.

What a difference between Wales and Ireland.

Here is a plain tale of persecution of Catholics in
Ballyclare, (Jtl), Antrim :—'* On the 9th and 10th July
the non-Catholic workers employed at Messrs. Kirk-
patrick Bros.” Bleach Works, Ballyclare, refused to
continue work until the Catholic workers, who number
only 27, were dismissed. At the request of those in
charge of the different departments the Catuolics agreed
to leave, expecting that after the usual holidays they
would be taken back again. However, on resumption
of work, it was evident that the feeling of opposition
still prevailed and that they were not to be allowed to
continue their work. They asked for a reason why they
were being dismissed, but could not receive any satis-
faction. All seemed to be aware as to what was to take
place but no one could give an answer. They were
directed to go to the office, where they received their
wages and a fortnight’s pay in lieu of notice. ~ This
they accepted, and, as they were leaving the place they
were greeted with cheers by the remaining workers.
The head of the firm was asked to use his influence and
authority in seeing these workers were reinstated. He
took no action. He could give no reason for their dis-
missal. The result is that all the Catholic workers,
numbering 27 (19 men and 8 girls) out of 500, are now
cleared out. Some of these have been upwards of
twenty years in the employment of tho firm and no
fault was ever found with them. As an instance, here.
is a copy of a letter one man received :—** The bearer
——— has been in our employment for the past 13
years as — He has a thorough knowledge of the
working and we always found him attentive to
his duties and obliging.—XKirkpatrick Bros., Limited.
J. J. Kirkpatrick, Director.” A number of them leave

The *‘ Trish Times ™’ puts on an air of virtue in an
editorial on objectionable plays. It says:—

At the Roman Catholic Congress, at Norwich
yesterday, Father Thomas Kelly had some very
severe things to say about the moral influence of
the modern stage. He added that the Roman
Catholic Church could not possibly countenance
plays which, by their unhealthy appeals to the
imagination, tend to demoralise both the actors
and the audience. There is much truth in Father
Kelly’s indictment, in so far as it applies to the
‘‘ box-office ”’ type of play. We have seen in
Dublin many plays of the musical-comedy class
which were frank and vulgar appeals to the sen-
suality that is in all of us. Such plays have no-
thing to commend them. They ure a grave source
of danger to the young. It is the business of all
the Churches to discourage them i every possible
way.

As it is the business of all Churches to discourage
-these plays, would not one be correct in asserting that
it is at least the business of the ‘‘ Irish Times” to
refrain from making revenue out of them by declining
to take their advertisements? YWhen we came on the
scene twelve years ago we found the ‘‘ Irish Times ’
not only advertising but puffing an indecent music hall
performance : and since that time it has never refused
the advertisement of any of these plays that it now
editorially says ‘‘ are a grave source of danger to the
young.”’

THE BELFAST “CIVIL WAR.”

The Belfast blackguards have overshot the marl,
and even the ‘‘ Irish Times,”’ that indirectly, if nobt
directly, has been giving them encouragement, has-
been brought to heel like a whipped cur; and like a
whipped cur it snarls and growls as it slinks away.
Silence could no longer be maintained, and we doubt 1f
Carson and Law and the others will be as free with
their tongues in the near future in preaching lawless-
ness. The ¢ Irish Times’ is a most contempt-
ible paper. It runs away with alacrity from the
rivet throwers and murderous cowards of Belfast when
the situation was precipitated, if we may put it that
way, in the House of Commons. His Majesty’s soldiers
are to be employed to curb the ‘“ loyalty *’ of the noble
champions of ‘ civil and religious liberty,”” and Carson,
Law, and the ¢ Irish-Times,” have not the perverted
pluck to tell the soldiers to mutiny. The blusterers
have bungled badly; the ‘ civil war’’ was proclaimed
too soon, and the.world has-had a sample of the Orange

Qn Wednesday of last week the *“ Irish Times™”’ was

two sentences i—

‘“ There can be no two opinions about the dis-
orders' in_the Belfast shipyards. Political enthu-
siasm and religious conviction cease to be respect-
able when they express themselves in cowerdly and
brutal assaults on minorities.”’

‘Why did not Arnott’s coward paper say that before it
was whipped and terrified into saying it. Tt goes on to
snarl, having been whipped into making the above be-
lated declaration. The blackguards, it says, were
** fired with political passion.” “What a pretty political’
party to belong to when it inspires followers to
“‘cowardly and brutal assaults,”” as the ‘“ Irish Times '’
has been compelled to phrase it.

Where is all the bluster about the King’s soldiers
never to be asked to deal with Orange ruffianism and
lawlessness now ? The ‘‘ Irish Times ’ says:—

The Chief Secretary announced yesterday that
three battalions of soldiers will be available to-day
for the picketing of the disturbed districts, and that
the local police force is being increased. The civi-
lised public opinion of Belfast, backed by these
resources, should be able to put a speedy end to
the present outburst of lawlessness.

*“ Alf Fox,”’ having been whipped to heel, loses what-
ever head it has and goes into hysterics. During the
last thirty years Ireland has groaned and dwindled and
bled under the heel of a bigoted ascendancy. Anti-Ivish
and anti-Catholic bigotry and hatred have been at the
throat of Ireland.  But the ‘‘ Irish Times’’ in its
{renzy says :—

What is the Nationalist Party’s record in regard
to the rights of Irish minorities ? For thirty years
it has trodden those rights under foot.

It is a wonder it did not go into details and declare that
the Nationalist Party selected the staffs of the Great
Northern Railway, the Provincial Bank, and other
public and private corporations and firms that it is un-
necessary for us to mention! When the ** 1rish Times’’
was whipped into denouncing the Belfast murderous
blackguards it was not equal to doing it with the out-
ward pretentions of a gentleman—it did the business
like a whipped cad. It has taken its beating badly. A
Mr. Barnes very sensibly complained that certain Eng-
lish Trades Unions have to pay unemployment benefits
to certain of the men rendered idle by murderous Belfast
blackguardism. Surely that is a sensible complaint.
Why should not an English or any other institution
complain of having to pay as a consequence of mur-
derous blackguards in Belfast not allowing 1ts members
to follow their work ? But the ‘‘ Irish Times,"” in its
state of unreasoning hysterics, says:— The most
damaging exposure of the -soul of the ‘great British
democracy,” to which Mr. Redmond has ificed so
much, was made by Mr. Barnes.”” And having set oub

*‘ The great British democracy will do anything for an
Irishman—so long as the kindness costs it nothing.*’
In this connection that comment is simply inane. What
complaint, can there be against anyone, English, Irish
or Japunese, who protests at having to pay money in
consequence of the lawless work of murderous black-
guards? °

The Generalissimo, Bonar Law of the shake hands,
and the ‘“ Irish Times,”” and all the rest of the con-
temptible gang who have been encouraging lawlessness,
divectly or indivectly, have had a bad fall. His Ma-
jesty’s soldiers have been ordered to look after the brave
defenders of ‘civil and keligious liberty,”” and the
““Trish Times ’’ winds up its article this way :—"‘ It is
the Unionist Party, and the Unionist Party alone, which
has a moral right to insist that these scandalous disor-
ders shall be suppressed with a strong hand.”  Thus
ends the first chapter of the Orange ** ¢ivil war.”

ALONG THE COAST.

TJ OLIDAY-MAKING is not so generally practised in

Ireland as it is across the Irish Sea. To speak
more precisely we should say holiday-taking. There s
in reality a world of difference between the two expres-
sions. To make holiday is to rejoice greatly once in a
way. To take holiday is to abandon work and, usually,
home for a more or less protracted period. An instance
will serve. Most pecple in obedience to the stern de-
cree of the Legislature make holiday on the nrst Monday
in August. Many people who can afford to do so—and
a great many. who can not—take holidays during some
weeks of the same month. This latter practice has
much to commend it and one hopes to see it more widely,
indulged in in this country. Owing to its prevalence in
Great Britain the newspapers in that island, actuated
by a brotherly spirit, are in the habit of printing day by,
day puaragraphs from seaside resorts giving particulars
as to the weather, the amusements, and the genergl
conditions obtaining at these places. Our Irish papers
actuated by a brotherly spirit of the dark variety do
nothing of the kind except when a stray ‘‘ad.”” requires
the supplement of a “‘puff.”” [We have learnt these
expressions from the Iiditor.—A.] Now there are
numerous delightful seaside places within easy reach of
Dublin which would be the better for a little ““cracking-
up.” Tt is our ambition to remedy the state of affairs
of which we have complained, and impelled by this
laudable motive, uninfluenced by thought of gain, we
malke haste to do so.

STRANDY SANDYMOUNT.

The season here is now in full swing. The splendid
strand stretching from the sea wall almost to the Pool-
beg Light is black with visitors—especially on Sundays.
Sandymount is-unique among seaside resorts by reason
of the almost perpetual absence of sea. In this respect;
it claims with pride the title of the driest watering-place
on the coast.

All the local amusements are well patronised, the
penny-in-the-slot machines being hardly equal to the

* demand. The bathing is largely availed of and is un-

deniably holesome. The Amusements Committee have
under consideration a scheme for improving the pier
and baths, but intending visitors would be well advised
not to defer their visit until the completion of these
plans.

Sandymount may be reached by tram or rail from
DUBLIN. Passengers by the latfer should be careful
to book to SYDNEY PARADE as, in order to meet the
convenience of travellers, the railway station at Sandy-
mount has been closed.

MERRY MERRION.

Those of quiet tastes, to whom the giddy distractions
of SANDYMOUNT make no appeal, will obtain at this
pretty seaside suburb the rest and repose they desire.
Bathers who scorn the artificial comforts of the ordinary
baths will find the open sea bathing along the sea wall
particularly pleasant. The disused rauway station
serves as a pavilion or dressingroom.  Visitors are
warned to bring with them their own towels—and soap.
Bands (of children) are a feature on Sundays. SANDY-
MOUNT itself is within easy reach of Merrion, as also
is BOOTERSTOWN—a pretty hamlet lower down along
the coast.

Visitors to Merrion should travel by tram or train.
Those who prefer steam to electric traction should be
careful to alight at SYDNEY PARADE as, with com-
mendable forethought, the Merrion railway station has
been diverted from its original purpose and has been
plﬁced at the convenience of bathers and trippers gene-
rally.

DELIGHTFUL DALKEY.

This fashionable resort may truthfully be said, in the
popular phrase, to be ‘‘ going strong.” Visitors keep
steadily pouring down, especially on Sundays before
two and after five p.m. To quote the words of one of
them, * for bona-fide enjoyment Dalkey is Al.”” The
harbours are thronged with sea-going craft. and it is
quite a pleasure to watch the busy fleet of excursion
boats plying between the mainland and Dalkey Island.
*“ The Island,’’ as it is known locally, continues to at-
tract numerous visitors. The Martello Tower, its pre-
mier edifice, is still without a rival.

- Drowning has not yet begun this year at the popular

sinking-place on Vico Road, but a brisk season is anti-
cipated later. Sorrento Park on Saturday evenings pre-
sents an undeniably Italian (warehouse) appearance.
The beautiful bay, which so forcibly recalls that of
Naples to those unfamiliar with the latter may be ad-
mired free of charge. Notwithstanding the abnormal
severity of the summer fully half an hour’s sunshine
was recorded at Dalkey last month. KILLINEY (1
mile) may be reached by foot or char-a-banc.

Dalkey is accessible by tram, train, or steamboat.
The last-mentioned modeé of transit is, however, mainly
availed of by day trippers.

" SUNNY SANDYCOVE.

There is now no accommodation to be had at hotels
or hydros here. Situated as it is Letween such large
centres as KINGSTOWN and DALKEY, Sandycove
(the seaside resort without a pier) provides a welcome
retreat for those who delight in otium cum dignitate.
By-laws have been rigidly enforced to bring about this
result.

Water-golf—40 (ft.) hole—which is, of course, the

* principal amusement, is extremely popular, and the

RIPPING RINGSEND.

Those who like to dwell far from the madding crowd
might do worse than visit this little town  Primarily a
fishing village, its proximity to DUBLIN has in no
small way imbuded it and its inhabitants with an at-
mosphere of civic culture. Among the chief buildings
are the Dogs’ Home and the Town Hall. ‘rhe latter is
admirably adapted for its functions as it was formerly
the Supper Room of the Dublin Mansion House. Since
the adoption of the Simple Life by Lord Mayors there
has, of course, ceased to be any necessity for such an
apartment in Dublin.

The sanitation of Ringsend is perfect, as may easily
be realised when it is understood that a sum running
into millions has been expended in connection with the
drainage operations at the Pigeon House Fort in the
neighbourhood. The romantic Dodder joins the Liffey
at Ringsend and the ‘‘ meeting of the waters’ is only
less beautiful than that at AVOCA.

Ringsend is conveniently situated for tourists on the
Dublin Tram Line and the Grand Canal.

CLINKING CLONTAREF'.

Facing RINGSEND, across the river, is the historic
whiskey-and-watering-place, Clontarf, which may in all
sincerity be said to be as gay as ever. Well has it
earned the title of ‘‘ Queen of the Sloblands.’ Bathers
here run very little risk of a wetting as the water at low
tide does not exceed an average of 1.2 inches. The
famous Green Lanes are among the chief attractions for
nature lovers. 8

. Those whose interest lies in historical associations de-
rive much pleasure from visiting the site of the cele-
brated battle with the Danes.  Antiquarians are able
to gratify their tastes by examining the wonders of
Conquer Hill. Tennis is in great vogue, and the golf
links at DOLLYMOUNT (tram 1d.) are well patronised.

Clontarf~has an excellent tram and train service.
Passengers to DUBLIN from the North will do well to
bear in mind that all TRAINS STOP AT CLONTARF
and that all tickets are takenup here

T HE saying has grown trite with time—‘‘ Ireland
4 sober is Ireland free.”’” Our enemies tell us, and
have been busy dinning it into the ears of the world,
that we are a drunken race. Statisticians tell us, and
statistics seem to prove it, that we are no worse than
-some of our neighbours in this respect. -But what good
Trishman will be satisfied with this comparison. To be
no worse than our neighbours is a negative boast as it
is a very negative consolation. We all gladly admib
that a very considerable improvement has talken place
in recent years, at least in rural Ireland, and more
especially in the public attitude to the drink question.
In our fairs through the country we no longer, or ravely,
see the mad driving of drunken drivers along our coun-
try roads; nor do we hear the bacchanalian roars that
formerly used to greet the ear from every tavern as one
passed along a village street on a fair evening. This
change for the better we all gladly recognise; but that
enormous drink bill, that appalling and undiminishing
«drain from an impoverished country, how account for
it? It is something of a paradox.

Myr. Begbie, that uncompromising foe of all things

Roman, had nothing but good things to say of us on a
recent visit. Iiven in the squalor of our Dublin slums
hie could see a light that was not elsewhere, and seemed
to have some faint grasp of what it w a reflex from
the purer air of the country, a pure faith that still shone
in filth and sin and misery. If ouwr Dublin slums can
find an apologist, how unutterably hideous must the
slums of the English industrial cities be! And when
we recollect that these must necessarily be the home of
large numbers of our race who left these shores devoid
of everything that should be the chief care of a paternal
government, and found themselves in a strange environ-
ment, a proud, sensitive race, surrounded by hatred
and prejudice, and worst of all, contempt, the wonder
is not that many have sunk to an unspeakable degra-
dation, but that so large a number have risen trium-
phant over circumstances so adverse. ~We have no
reason to feel ashamed of our race; they have filled and
1fill a place in history out of all proportion to the size
of the country and its resources, and which, when we
take into consideration all the circumstances of our
history, has in it something of the miraculous. Still,
what have we got to do with other countries? Why
should we find any great satisfaction in the fact that
we are no worse than others who, from a material point
of view at any rate, have much more to squander. The
bald fact remains staring us in the face from out the
pages of history, and in the living present, that the
-drink habit has been almost our only great vice, and
certainly our greatest curse. This very truth is en-
shrined in one of our national songs :—

““ We bravely fought and conquered at Ross and Wex-
ford town, 5
And if we failed to keep them, ’twas drink that brought

__us.down.”

The political aspect is most hopeful at the present
time, but it is not the only hopeful sign. Politics are
the restless waves that forever rise and subside, and
are sometimes erratic like the .winds that blow over
‘them. Deep down in the national life many forces are
at work that must eventually result in the regeneration
of this country no matter how Cabinets may come and
go. The Gaelic League has done remarkable and last-
ing work which bears fruit in many departments of our

I had an interesting chat some shorb time ago with a
young priest on the English Mission, one of that large
number of noble young missionaries we send out to till
the vineyard of the Lord far afield to the uttermost ends
of the earth. They see our race at its best and—at its
worst : filling important positions, increasing every day
in power and influence, and well able to hold their own
with any race on earth. But they also see where the
vilest of this earth herd many a child of St. Patrick,
differing alone perhaps from the human beasts that
surround them in an indefinable something, a subtle
air of grace, a dim memory of far-away innocent days,
or the bright light of a long line of pure ancestry nov
wholly extinguished. Keenly alive to the sad hayoe
wrought among his poorer flock, the late distinguished
prelate, his Iiminence Cardinal Manning, started a
great Temperance crusade, the good work of which is
still bearing abundant fruit; and which has since given
birth to many societies all having for thew object a
common goal, the promotion of temperance. One of
the most notable of these is the Father Mathew Union,
a society for total-abstaining priests for the promotion
of temperance. There are many societies amiong the
priests in London, for example, the Soggarth Society
(composed exclusively of native born Irish priests), and
the Sick Benefit Society, but this is unique in its way.
As its name would seem to imply, the majority of its
members are Irish. It is under the patronage of his
Eminence the Cardinal Archbishop of Westminster and
the Bishops of Southwark and Arindella, and has for
its president Canon Murnane, one of the best known
and best beloved priests in London. It has for secra-
tary Monsignor Cologan, president of the Catholic
Truth Society of England, and the author of a well-
known book on temperance. Like its prototype, the
Total Abstinence Society of Ireland, which has done
such noble work under the presidency of Father Cullen,
the Father Mathew Union lays special stress on carry-
ing on the propaganda in the ranks of the young
Levites, whose future attitude to the drink evil is neces-
sarily of supreme importance. To this view a lecture
was delivered last year at St. John’s Seminary, Won-
ersh, by Monsignor Cologan, and a substantial prize
awarded to the student writing the best essay on the
subject. This year a similar course has been pursued
at St. Bdmund’s Seminary, Ware, the lecturer being
Father George Newton, known through the length and
breadth of London for the past twenty years as a pro-
minent lecturer on the Temperance cause. Next year
the members of the Union contemplate holding an inter-
collegiate contest between St. John's and St. Edmund’s
to stimulate interest in the subject among the students.
Thus has the Union extended its operations from very
small beginnings; and we who live in the homeland
must view with entire satisfaction the work of any such
society whose object is the betterment of our exiled
children, for I strongly hold this view: No single unit
of our widely scattered race is wholly independent of
the others; they act and re-act on one another. The
higher we rise (and we are beginning to look up) at
home, the more will our children in other lands be dis-
posed to hold up their heads and demean themselves as
becomss members of a proud and self-respecting race;
and the more our children prosper and grow strong in
other lands, the more self-reliance shall we gather, a
gunli(,y naturally much needed in a land long sick unto

eath.

. The term Intermediate is rather a modern one, and

great majority of our readers to be allowed to state that
it signifies the mid part of the educational field, lying

In 1878 the Tory Government: of the day recognised

Time there was, little more than two generations

All the magnificent endowments, which the piety of

education, and the outcome of which is so eloquently
d‘esc'nbod by Dr. Healy in his magnificent work on
¢ Irish Schqo]s and Scholars,”” had all been annexed by
the other Irish nation, not through their own prowess,

Coming back to 1878, it was felt, by the niggardly

and Lord Ashbourne, and probably behind him, the son

The task was, to level up the position of the Papists
to that of the settlers; the simple way to do it was %o
give us endowments proportionate to the public ones
held by Protestants. ~These public endowments were
confined to one of the sects, viz., the Episcopalian one !

A committee of Irish Catholics had investigated with
great care the endowments derived in cne way or an-
other from public sources, applicable to Intermediate
education, and in possession of the one Protestant sect
which had them. :

They found that the half million of Episcopalians
enjoyed the handsome income of £50,000 a year for
Intermediate purposes. It is unnecessary to state that
this came directly or indirectly from Catholic property
or Catholic possessions.

Under ordinary circumstances Irish Catholics would
have been offered the alternative which Mr. Disraeli
praetically offered us in 1866, when our condition was
to have been levelled up by a State endowment for the
clergy, or as the Whig party wanted, and ultimately
carried, by having all endowments taken away from the
dominant party. It would have been better for us
Catholics, and better for our opponents, if the levelling
up process had been adopted in the educational field.
The Presbyterians, for instance, would have got for
themselves those local schools, which their representa-
tives afterwards tried to get for themselves.

The fanatical minority, however, who, then as now,
and then as always, were so drunk with fanaticism; as
to be incapable of seeing even the length of their noses,
opposed denominational education tooth and nail, and
with the result that this blind minority successfully
opposed a settlement, which would have been as muci
for their own interests as for ours. .

" the entire middle class population. It acted, howevar,

like a constant electric shock on the staffs and the pupils
of the greab schools, which, for perhaps the first time,
were converted into busy hives of unceasing intellectual
labour. The Catholics came out so splendidly at the
examinations that, for the first time, they were enabled
to measure the relative worth of themselves and their
opponents. Up to that, even educated Protestants
fanned themselves with the pleasing delusion that the
mere Papist was nowhere and that they themselves pos-
sessed all the knowledge, as they undoubtedly did, all
the power of the nation.

A fair stand-up fight, conducted year after year under
unimpeachable ~auspices, revealed the intellectual
powers and possessions of both Protestant and Catholic
and made an end of insolent pretensions which were
without any foundation, save that which had its origin
in ignorance and fanaticism. This system has now
been at work for years—it has not levelled up Catholics,
it has not provided cheap education for the struggling
middle classes, but it has, without doubt, made num-
bers of youths and the teachers of these youths, hard-
working and filled with a respectable amount of know-
ledge. On the other hand, the examinational system
has run away with all the faculties of the mind but that
of memory aloue, and no sooner is the examination over
than with numbers, at any rate, the knowledge was in
at one ear and out at the other. I have interrogated
youths of undoubted abilities and industry, and their
replies were that three months after the examinations
they have forgotten all about them. I can speak fromn
personal knowledge of whole presses of the daintiest
text-books flung aside as you would fling faded flowers—
no longer to be thought of.

Contrast this with the reyerence with which an older
generation treated their Homers and their Ciceros.
Why the Lares and Penates were not in it with the love
and reverence extended ‘to these trophies of 'bygone
years. At the present moment there is a growing
volume of opinion ‘against the whole system, and in
face of that I maintain that the proper course to adopt
is to appoint a small committee on which the public
as well as the schoolmasters would be represented, to
investigate the working of the whole system. Mr.
Birrell is now several years Chief Secretary for Ireland,
and T have no hesitation in stating that he is as guileless
to-day as to the real character of the bigots who con-
stitute his official environment as he was the first day
he entered on office. If he were not, he would never
propose to give the unrestricted disposal of public funds,
as he now does, to any Castle Board in Ireland.

A goodly number of, shall I venture to say, hack
teachers are at work in both Catholic and Protestant
Secondary institutions.

Many of these hold degrees and are men of mark.
All of them are industrious, plodding. hard-working,
men. As assistants they perform useful work, and mn
the nature of things they are liable to removal and in
the case of the Catholic schools are dealing with people

_who, to put it mildly, are not over-troubled with worldly
gear. These gentlemen have an association, and the
Protestant and Catholic head masters have hkewise an
association each.

A claim has been put forward by these teachers for
increased remuneration, which was reasonable enough.
They have, however, advanced the further claim of

fixity of tenure, which would be as admissible as it
would be for the assistants in a great business estab-
lishment, or for the non-commissioned officers of a
regiment.

The Catholic head masters, and likewise the Protes-
tant ones, in their desperate anxiety to escape from an
untenable. pecuniary position, have grasped at straws
and have thrust forward their poorly-paid dependents
as the stalking horses who are to convey them to their
much-desired ~ destination. Certain indiscreet advo-
cates of the assistants have been parading them in the
newspapers as the assistant teachers, and indeed ‘‘ the
secondary teachers’ of Ireland, forgetting, not merely
the modest rolé they play, but that for instance every
man in Blackrock or Clongowes, after the Superior of
each of those great institutions, is simply an assistant
teacher and the lay helps fill up the hinder ranks with
all the assertion that they can command. One result
of this would undoubtedly be that the public would
come to look upon the modest helps as the chief mer
and in a short time Catholic Ireland would cease fo
reckon the great clerical body as the Alpha and Omega
of the whole system.

The indiscretion of this attitude was, and is, so ob-
vious and so great that it is impossible to enterfain
much respect for their foolish counsellors so palpably
unlearned in the law—of common sense. I invited the
most obtrusive advocate of these gentlemen to define
the demarcations of secondary education and to tell the
public what are the qualifications of his clients to under-
take the rolé of teachers in such a field 7 But to that
1 received no reply. Now it is proposed to introduce
one-clause Bill handing over certain moneys to the
Intermediate Commissioners with unrestricted powers
to divide these amongst the schools.

This proposition will upset the whole principle on
which the Intermediate Act is founded, will place the

unendowed Catholic only on the same level as
the endowed Protestant, though in equity a
sum of three hundred thousand pounds a year

should be 4t once given to Catholics to enable them to
start on equal terms with the endowed Episcopalian
Protestants.  Is this legitimate, is it fair play for
Catholics, is it just to them ? The conduet of certain
Catholic head masters, in bowing to the Orange dictum,
which demanded that the terms *‘Assistant,”” *‘Master
should be confined to laymen alone is so pusillanimous
and so reprehensible that I feel an utter want of words
with which to adequately denounce it. It would ap-
pear that at least some of them were unaware of the
labours of the Catholic Committee, which some years
ago laboriously and minutely investigated the whole
matter and found that the small Episcopalian body of
a half million souls were in possession of no less than
this £50,000 a year of public endowments. I
raise an emphatic protest as one layman
against the follies of these Catholic head masters
being crowned with success. They don’t realize the
far-reaching consequences of these follies. There
something very mean in the duplicity of the Protestans
head masters. The religious bodies they represent op-
posed tooth and nail the legitimate claims of Catholics
to direct endowment for secondary education. It was
owing to them that a new-fangled and utterly untried
system was introduced, and now they would fain sneal
in under cover of their very accommodating Catholic
brethren and accept that very denominational endow-
ment they so vehemently opposed at an earlier date.
Heve the Catholic public any voice left in public mat-
ters, because if they have, the sooner they make that
voice heard in so vital a public matter the better will
it be for all parties concerned.
